title: The print() Function 🖨️
description: Master Python's most essential output tool.

slides.title: print() — Your Best Friend 🤝
slides.content:
# The print() Function

You've already used `print()` — now let's master it!

The `print()` function is the **most used function** in Python. It's your window into understanding what your program is doing. Whenever you want to see a result, check a value, or display information, `print()` is your go-to tool.

slides.title: Printing Multiple Items 📋
slides.content:
## Commas to the Rescue!

You can print **multiple items** in a single `print()` call by separating them with **commas**. Python will automatically add a **space** between each item:

```
print("Name:", "Alice")
```

**Output:** `Name: Alice`

You can even mix text and numbers:

```
print("Age:", 25)
```

**Output:** `Age: 25`

The comma acts like a separator, and Python handles the spacing for you! ✨

slides.title: Try It Out! 🧪
slides.content: See how commas let you print multiple things at once:
slides.codeSnippet:
print("Hello", "World")
print("I am", 10, "years old")
print("Score:", 95, "out of", 100)

quizData.question: What does print("Hi", "there") output?
quizData.explanation: When you use commas in print(), Python automatically inserts a space between each item. So it outputs: Hi there

slides.title: The sep Parameter 🔗
slides.content:
## Custom Separators

By default, `print()` uses a **space** to separate items. But you can change this with the `sep` parameter:

```
print("2025", "03", "15", sep="-")
```

**Output:** `2025-03-15`

The `sep` parameter replaces the default space with any string you choose:

* `sep="-"` → uses dashes
* `sep="/"` → uses slashes
* `sep=""` → no separator at all
* `sep="❤️"` → even emojis work!

slides.title: Experimenting with sep ⚡
slides.content: Try different separators and see the results:
slides.codeSnippet:
print("A", "B", "C", sep="-")
print("Hello", "World", sep="***")
print("No", "Spaces", sep="")

quizData.question: What is the output of: print("X", "Y", sep="+")?
quizData.explanation: The sep="+" parameter replaces the default space separator with a plus sign. So X and Y are joined by +, giving X+Y.

slides.title: The end Parameter 🔚
slides.content:
## Control What Happens After print()

By default, `print()` adds a **newline** (`\n`) at the end — that's why each `print()` appears on a new line. You can change this with the `end` parameter:

```
print("Hello", end=" ")
print("World!")
```

**Output:** `Hello World!` (on one line!)

The `end` parameter controls what comes after the printed text. Set it to `" "` to keep printing on the same line, or to anything else you'd like!

slides.title: end in Action 🎬
slides.content: Watch how `end` changes the output behavior:
slides.codeSnippet:
print("Loading", end="...")
print("Done!")
print()
print("A", end=" -> ")
print("B", end=" -> ")
print("C")

quizData.question: What does the `end` parameter in print() control?

options.text: The text at the beginning of the output
The character(s) printed after the output

quizData.explanation: The `end` parameter controls what is printed at the very end of the print() output. By default it's a newline (\n).

slides.title: Escape Characters 🚪
slides.content:
## Special Characters Inside Strings

Sometimes you need to include special characters inside strings. Python uses **backslash** `\` as an escape character:

* `\n` — **Newline** (moves to the next line)
* `\t` — **Tab** (adds a big space / indent)
* `\\` — **Backslash** (prints a literal `\`)
* `\'` — **Single quote** inside single-quoted strings
* `\"` — **Double quote** inside double-quoted strings

slides.title: Escape Characters in Action 🎯
slides.content: See how escape characters create special formatting:
slides.codeSnippet:
print("Line 1\nLine 2")
print("Name:\tAlice")
print("She said \"Hello!\"")
print("Path: C:\\Users\\Python")

quizData.question: What does \n do inside a string?
quizData.explanation: The escape sequence \n creates a newline — it moves the cursor to the next line in the output.
